values.detail: Year-round burning intensifies. Savanna fire season extends by 3+ months by 2070.
Tropical-temperate interface. Fire frequency doubles under high emissions.
Wet-dry season boundary shifts south. Rainforest edges increasingly threatened.
Heatwaves of 50°C+ projected. Fire risk index nearly triples.
Desert spinifex fires become more intense and frequent under drier conditions.
Rainfall down 20%. Perth Hills and Darling Range face near-annual catastrophic seasons.
Declining winter rainfall extends fire weather into autumn and spring.
2005 Wangary fire scenario becomes 3x more likely. Grain-growing regions at acute risk.
Rocky terrain and dry scrub. Fire weather days increase by 130% by 2070.
Hinterland ranges at risk of repeated burning. Koala habitat under severe pressure.
New England and Northern Rivers — fire seasons extend into winter months.
80% burned in Black Summer. Near-annual catastrophic conditions projected by 2060.
Bega Valley and South Coast — recovery from 2020 cut short by shortening fire cycles.
Alpine ash forests fire-stressed. Return interval drops below 15 years — extinction threshold.
Leadbeater's Possum last habitat under repeated fire threat.
Grampians and Pyrenees — fire weeks extend into autumn and spring.
90% probability of a Cudlee Creek-scale event by 2050.
49% burned in 2020. Unique fauna projected to face repeat burns within 20 years.
Ancient pencil pine forests burning for first time in millennia.
World Heritage rainforest. 2016 fires were unprecedented — becoming recurring.
2003 reached suburbs. 2020 surrounded the territory. Near-annual extreme risk by 2060.
Otways and Portland — plantation forests face increased risk through 2050s.
Cultural burning traditions under pressure as fire seasons grow beyond traditional management windows.
Savanna grasslands face intensifying fire cycles. Cattle station operations impacted.
Agricultural heartland. Drought-fire coupling increases crop loss risk significantly by 2050.
Critically endangered ecosystem. Already fragmented — repeated fire would be catastrophic.
High-altitude ecosystems with no fire-adapted species. Snowpack decline compounds fire risk.
Wine regions and coal country. Fire weather season extends through autumn.
Irrigation agriculture at risk. Murray-Darling Basin fire risk rises with reduced flows.
Flat, wind-exposed country. Fast-moving grassland fires increasingly likely by 2040.
Dry farming landscape. Late-season fire risk rising as summers extend.
Semi-arid horticulture zone. Fire frequency increase driven by cyclone-rainfall variability.
Pastoral country. Seasonal fire risk extended by earlier dry-season onset.
Semi-arid scrubland. Mining operations face increased fire exposure windows.
Ancient, slow-growing vegetation. Any fire impact is effectively permanent on human timescales.
One of Australia's last true rainforest refugia. Increasingly vulnerable to dry-season incursion.
World Heritage rainforest. Fire not historically present — now encroaching at margins.
World's largest sand island. 2020 fire burned 87,000 ha. Return interval now under 5 years.
Coastal scrubland and reef catchments. Fire risk rising with La Niña cycle disruption.
Oldest rainforest on Earth. Historically fire-free — first recorded fires appeared post-2016.
Coastal monsoon forests. Extended dry seasons push fire deeper into previously immune vegetation.
Grain-growing plains. Fast-spreading grass fires during harvest season increasingly catastrophic.
Burned catastrophically in 2019-20. Recovery habitat for Eastern Bristlebird — uniquely at risk.
